game.opening: Queen's Pawn Game, Queen's Knight Defence

engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: Grabbed the c6-pawn and lost a knight in the wash
mistakes.explanation: Black's 19…Ba6 hit your bishop on d3 — the quiet 20.Bc2 saves it, and with the b4-pawn guarding the knight on a5, nothing hangs. Instead 20.Rxc6 pocketed a pawn and left the rook stranded deep in Black's camp: after 20…Bxe3 21.Qxe3 Bxd3 22.Qxd3 came the desperado 22…Rxa5! — 23.bxa5 Qxc6 and the queen scooped the loose rook, netting Black a knight for a pawn. You were still winning, but two of your pieces vanished from an attack that wanted them.

takeaways.lesson: Answer the threat before grabbing material.
takeaways.detail: …Ba6 attacked your d3 bishop, and a capture somewhere else doesn't make that threat go away. After every enemy move, first ask what it now attacks — deal with that, then go shopping.

takeaways.lesson: A rook deep in enemy territory needs a way home.
takeaways.detail: Before landing a rook on a square like c6, count the enemy pieces that can hit it and check its retreat squares. If the forced trades end with the rook still there and undefended, it is usually just lost.

mistakes.title: The a1-rook never joined the game
mistakes.explanation: By move 25 your pawn stood on a6 and Black's queen was single-handedly holding the queenside — the moment to bring in your last piece. 25.Rb1 attacks the queen on b6 with tempo, and after 25…Nh6 26.Rxb6 cxb6 27.Qb5+ the a-pawn queens by force. Instead 25.Qa3 kept manoeuvring the queen alone (the engine had already wanted Rb1 the move before, instead of 24.a6). It still won — but your a1-rook literally never moved in this game, and conversions go fastest when every piece pulls.

takeaways.lesson: Bring your worst piece before starting the final push.
takeaways.detail: Before forcing matters, scan for any piece still on its home square and give it one move toward the action. A whole rook on a1 is a whole rook missing from the attack.

takeaways.lesson: Put rooks on open files where they hit something.
takeaways.detail: Rb1 gains a tempo on the b6-queen and takes the only open file. A rook move that comes with a threat costs nothing — the opponent must answer it while your plan rolls on.

mistakes.title: Pushed past a pawn that was free to take
mistakes.explanation: 3…d5 simply hung a pawn: 4.exd5 wins it outright, and the new d5-pawn even attacks the knight on c6 — if 4…Qxd5, then 5.Nc3 kicks the queen and you develop with tempo. The engine's line runs 4.exd5 Na7 5.c4: a clean extra pawn and a huge centre. 4.e5 looks imposing, but it closed the position, handed d5 back for nothing, and shrank a +2.6 edge to under +1.

takeaways.lesson: Check every capture before every push.
takeaways.detail: Run the forcing moves in order — checks, captures, threats — before considering quiet advances. Here the very first capture on the list won a pawn on the spot.

takeaways.lesson: A recapture that walks into tempo is no defence.
takeaways.detail: If the only way to take back is with the queen stepping into a developing attack (4…Qxd5 5.Nc3), the pawn was never really defended. Count the sequence to its end, not just the first exchange.

mistakes.title: A tempo on the rim while the centre break waited
mistakes.explanation: Black had just retreated a knight all the way back to g8 — the position was begging for 11.c4, striking at d5 while half of Black's army sat on its starting squares. 11.Na3 spent that tempo parking your own knight on the edge, where it aimed at nothing Black couldn't cover. You played 12.c4 a move later anyway, so the knight move was a pure pause — the eval dipped a full pawn and Black got …h5 in for free.

takeaways.lesson: When your opponent wastes moves, open the position at once.
takeaways.detail: A lead in development is a melting asset — it only converts while lines are opening. The moment the opponent un-develops or shuffles, look for the pawn break, not a slow improving move.

takeaways.lesson: Ask what a knight will do from the rim before sending it there.
takeaways.detail: If the honest answer to 'where does it go next?' is a square the opponent easily controls, keep the knight central and spend the tempo on the break instead.

mistakes.title: Mate in one — missed twice
mistakes.explanation: With two queens against a bare king anything wins, but on move 34 Qc8# ended it on the spot: the queen slides behind the king, the f6-queen covers e7 and d8, and f8 stays on the checking rank. You played 34.Qc6+ instead — and a move later passed on 35.Qa8# too, before 36.Qce7# finally landed. No harm done here, but the habit matters: against a defender with real resources, every extra move in a won position is another chance for a stalemate trick or a loose piece. When the king is naked, hunt the mate — don't just check.

takeaways.lesson: When the enemy king is bare, look for mate before you check.
takeaways.detail: Scan every checking move and ask 'does the king have a single square left?' before playing the first check you see. Mate-in-one patterns are pure habit — they only fire if you look.
Sloppy finishing costs nothing against a bare king and everything against a swindler: stalemates and perpetuals live in careless won positions. Practise converting with the fastest mate, every time.

highlights.note: 8.Bb5 developed with a threat against the c6-knight while Stockfish shuffled its bishop backwards — a new piece out on nearly every move is why you were clearly better by move 10.
The winning habit your other games have been missing: at +3 you struck with 12.c4 to open lines for your pieces instead of sitting on the advantage. 16% of your level plays it.

openingReport.note: 1.d4 Nc6 2.Nf3 left known theory with a perfectly sound developing move — no harm done. The first improvable decision was 4.e5 (72.8% → 59.1%), pushing past a capture that was simply free: 4.exd5 wins a clean pawn. Check every central capture before every push.
